The glacier is absolutely worth seeing; the actual sight is incredibly breathtaking and worth every penny;
The local roasted lamb is excellent; it’s a paradise for carnivores;
When flying in South America, always leave plenty of buffer time;
Argentina maintains a high level of focus and widespread commemoration regarding the Malvinas Islands (known as the Falkland Islands in the UK).
💨 “Betrayal” After the Initial Plan Shattered Originally, several friends and I had been saying for a long time that we wanted to travel through Argentina together. But in a group full of “P-type” personalities (MBTI), only one person actually did any planning—a polite, hardworking Singaporean friend who carefully listened to everyone’s ideas. Due to various factors, we eventually scrapped our original plan for a self-drive tour through El Calafate and El Chaltén, deciding instead to visit Iguazu Falls first and then head to Ushuaia together.
However, looking back at that decision, the conclusion to skip El Calafate seems absurd. Ironically, most of us ended up succumbing to “FOMO” (Fear Of Missing Out) and went to see the glaciers anyway, despite our schedules being last-minute and convoluted.
I was the first one to “betray” the group, for which I was mercilessly mocked by my friends. Before the trip, I had argued in our group chat that El Calafate and the glacier tours were too expensive, suggesting that if time was limited, we should prioritize Bariloche and Ushuaia instead. It’s true that flights from BA to El Calafate and the tour prices are high, but the glacier really is worth it. Perito Moreno is special because it’s one of the few “living” glaciers still advancing. Plus, after being “sold” on it by a video from the creator Video Hurricane, I was willing to let my wallet take the hit.
Anyway, the group had originally agreed to give up on El Calafate, and I was undoubtedly the one who took the “fall” for that decision. The final consensus was: aside from Iguazu and Ushuaia, all other stops would be arranged individually.
🧊 An Impulse Move: The Call of the Glacier Well, humans are followers. During our days in Buenos Aires, two of my friends (the “R-sisters”) left for four days to visit El Calafate. When they returned, seeing their photos and hearing their descriptions of the scenery made my heart itch. Combined with the fact that life in BA felt a bit monotonous, my desire for the outdoors grew stronger. As it turns out, I wasn’t the only one suffering from FOMO; “Teacher G,” a middle-aged gentleman in our group, had it even worse than I did. So, one night:
G: “Wanna go?”
Me: “Go where?”
G: “El Calafate. The glaciers. If we miss this chance, who knows when the next one will be? I heard it’s mind-blowing. We have to go!”
Me: “But it’s so expensive T_T”
G: “Hey, we’re already here! Wouldn’t it be a shame? The scenery is definitely worth it. Time is tight, buy the tickets now and we can still make it back in time.”
…
After a few rounds of this, I completely abandoned my stance and bought the tickets immediately (the price of being a “Special Forces” traveler—last-minute tickets are indeed pricey). For the next three days, the round-trip flight cost $580. I would spend one afternoon, one morning, and one full day in El Calafate—roughly 48 hours. Once I calmed down, my foreign friend teased and scolded me: “You said you weren’t going, and now you are? You let G give you FOMO 😓.” I was speechless—my friend’s Chinese isn’t great, so he doesn’t catch the nuances; from now on, I need to be direct with “yes” or “no.”
⛰️ In El Calafate We flew with Aerolineas Argentinas, but it was delayed, so we arrived an hour and a half late—checking into our hotel around 4:30 PM.
A word of advice: when flying in South America, leave as much buffer time as possible and keep a close eye on flight status. I have to vent about South American airlines again—delays are the norm, and everyone claps when the plane finally lands. Aerolineas Argentinas is only “good” depending on who you compare them to. Even though we didn’t have checked luggage (just one backpack each), we faced massive lines for check-in at AEP airport because their website crashed and the self-service kiosks weren’t working. The return trip was equally frustrating; El Calafate airport had no self-service machines and didn’t support online check-in, so we were forced to wait in a long queue.
Photo: The check-in line from El Calafate back to BA
El Calafate is great for driving, and there are rental counters at the airport, but since neither of us had processed our licenses, we skipped it. We took a shuttle bus from the airport to the hotel, which cost 12,000 Pesos one way. We were a bit slow; we should have bought the round-trip bus ticket since we already had our return flight booked. A round-trip ticket is cheaper—20,000 Pesos—but you can only pay in cash 💰.
Don’t even think about getting an Uber; it’s mostly taxis. I heard there are only four Uber drivers in the whole town 😂. Since we were late in booking our return shuttle, it was fully booked, and we were forced to take a taxi. The hotel staff gave me a taxi driver’s contact, and he quoted 33,000 Pesos to the airport. Later, I asked the “R-sisters,” and they only paid 30,000 😑.
🪄 Summary: If you are alone, book the round-trip bus at the airport 🚌; if there are 2 or more people, take a taxi 🚕.
The shuttle is a van with room for luggage, has heating, and is quite comfortable. While waiting, I met two Slovakian guys who were also on a “Special Forces” trip from BA. Seeing them wearing only shorts, I couldn’t help but ask if they were cold. Turns out, they were! 🤣 Interestingly, we chatted about the tight housing market in BA over the weekend. One guy told me he had booked his BA accommodation a year in advance just to see a concert, so his price was very low. I truly admired that level of planning.
El Calafate in mid-November is still very cold, especially with the high winds. A windproof outer layer and thermal inner layers are essential. It was cloudy the day we arrived. G was ambitious and wanted to book a tour immediately, but the reality of howling winds and rain made that impossible. We dropped our stuff at the hotel and headed out to find food and a tour.
Photo: Just got on the bus; dark clouds over the town, a sense of vastness and desolation
Photo: On the bus, heading to the hotel
Maybe because mid-November isn’t peak season, I found the hotel prices quite reasonable. I booked on Agoda, about a 7-minute walk from the town’s main street. I recommend staying near the main street for easier access to food. The town center is small enough that most places are convenient. The “R-sisters” stayed at a five-star hotel but felt it wasn’t really necessary. It makes sense—just like when I visited the Maldives, you spend most of your time outside, so as long as the hotel is livable, it’s fine.
One thing: if you’re taking the airport shuttle, I don’t recommend an Airbnb. Stick to hotels. Airbnbs can be tricky for shuttle pickups, whereas every shuttle driver knows the local hotels by name.
The hotel turned out to be quite nice, costing about 350 RMB per night. G and I each had a family-sized room with full facilities. The only issues were the weak Wi-Fi and the dim lighting—the latter seems to be a common theme in South America. Even in our BA apartments, the lights were mostly dim yellow—great for sleeping, but terrible for working.
Because of the glaciers and its location in Patagonia, El Calafate is a dedicated tourist town, so the industry is very well-developed. The town center is packed with travel agencies offering glacier tours. Prices vary slightly depending on the package, but they are generally consistent with market rates, with some agencies offering small discounts.
In the end, we booked with the agency the “R-sisters” recommended because their office looked more professional, and we thought their boats might be more luxurious. We booked a boat tour plus a basic walking tour on the shore. With the agency discount, it came to about 265,000 Pesos per person.
Photo: The boat we took
Side note: while booking, I learned that the name “El Calafate” comes from the Calafate bush. It’s a hardy, thorny shrub (Berberis buxifolia) with yellow flowers and deep blue berries often used for jam.
The guy at the agency gave us a drink made from these berries; it tasted like a sweet mix of honey and jam. He told us: “Whoever eats the Calafate berry will inevitably return here.” While it sounds more like a beautiful legend about the land’s charm than a strict prophecy, I do find this land incredibly captivating. I’ll likely go back if I get the chance.
G and I were lucky. Although it was rainy the afternoon we arrived, we paid our money and went to rest with heavy hearts. The next day, however, was perfectly clear and sunny. I felt like I had used up all my good luck for November right then! It’s an hour’s drive from the town to the pier, but the scenery is incredible. The richness of the Patagonian dry steppe ecosystem looks dazzling under the bright sun.
Photo: The distant lake looks blue due to the sunlight
Photo: From the bus window—the wind blows through the grass (but no cows or sheep in sight)
Only people from the south understand the excitement of seeing snow, let alone a glacier. The last time my threshold for natural scenery was raised was at Jade Dragon Snow Mountain, but this time, the bar was raised even higher. It was funny: at first, everyone was ecstatic. Almost the entire boat was on the deck, snapping photos of even the smallest, scattered icebergs. By the return trip, there were only a few people left on deck; the scattered ice could no longer hold anyone’s interest. Human nature—I was no different.
Photo: Some scattered ice appearing on the lake at first, though they are likely deep underwater
Photo: Encountering larger ice chunks; my megalophobia flared up as we got closer
Photo: Not sure why the top left is black; perhaps silt was trapped when it formed?
Our tour stopped at Puesto Las Vacas, where the trees are massive with gnarled branches. Even so, many were snapped by lightning or uprooted, making me shudder at the thought of the past weather here. Standing among those trees was pure awe.
The place is called Puesto Las Vacas, which means “Cow Post.” As soon as we stepped onto the grass, we found cow dung everywhere. The guide later told us that the cows were originally brought in from outside. Their numbers weren’t high initially, but because they have no natural predators here and the grass is excellent, the population exploded.
This led to ecological damage: the cows overgraze the grass, leading to soil degradation and erosion. Due to high transportation costs and bureaucratic red tape, the cow problem remains difficult to solve, much to the frustration of the local government.
Photo: Marveling at nature’s wonders and the powerful vitality of plants
Photo: The lake’s color, formed by the high altitude, is strikingly similar to Blue Moon Valley at Jade Dragon Snow Mountain
Our tour claimed to show three glaciers, but we only got close to two: Perito Moreno and Glaciar Spegazzini.
Glaciar Spegazzini in the distance. I stood at the bow here and got blasted by the wind like an idiot
Behind it is: Cerro Peineta
A quick word on the boats—facilities vary by company. Ours had three levels: the first had a restaurant, restrooms, and seating; the second was all seating; and the third was the bridge and deck. The boat provided free Wi-Fi and the heating was strong.
The restaurant sold pastries, coffee, and alcohol at prices almost identical to those in town. Given Argentina’s current inflation, it felt “friendly” to tourists. The only issue was the limited number of restrooms and long lines. Later, on our Ushuaia boat, there were plenty of restrooms and cheap food, but no Wi-Fi.
Captured from the boat—truly too beautiful!!
Oh, and the entrance fee for Los Glaciares National Park is separate and not included in the tour. We paid by card at the entrance—45,000 Pesos per person. So, including the boat tour, the glacier trip cost about 310,000 Pesos per person, which is roughly ¥1550. Since I might only do this once, I found the price acceptable, even though it has risen significantly compared to the past.
Full view of Perito Moreno—from the boat
Perito Moreno—from the catwalks; you can occasionally hear the distant crack of the glacier
The restaurants in town are mostly near the main street, along with souvenir shops. I bought a sheep 🐑 magnet because the design and quality were nice, for 4500 Pesos.
I suggest bringing both cash and a credit card to El Calafate as a double insurance. Mercado Pago is also a good choice if you have it, but prioritize cash. Most restaurants prefer cash and some even offer a 10% discount for cash payments. Argentina is a tipping country, and restaurants in El Calafate expect it, usually around 10%.
Photo: The sign in the middle says 10% off for cash payments
El Calafate has unique local food influenced by the Andes, the Patagonian terroir, and European immigrants (especially Italians and Spaniards). It focuses on highland livestock, wild ingredients, and charcoal grilling—a carnivore’s heaven.
Photo: But for me, the beef is still better
Photo: Table bread + a dip made from some kind of bean
The roasted lamb (Cordero Patagónico) is famous here. It’s actually a type of lamb I can actually eat (I’ve tried hotpot lamb, “Cangshu” lamb in Suzhou, and lamb in Turkey, but usually find the gamey smell—“shan wei”—overpowering and nauseating). Here, the gamey smell is only about 3%. I suspect I’m genetically sensitive to lamb, so even at 3%, I could only eat a little.
Photo: A massive portion; the tray almost spanned the entire table
Trout and salmon are also local specialties, but to be honest, I’m not a fan of the heavy cream and mashed potato preparation. It was too rich, and I couldn’t even finish the fish, regretting my choice. We also ordered Guanaco meat, a local specialty.
A huge plate, but so rich I couldn’t finish it
Source Wikipedia: Guanaco
Photo: Three ways of preparing Guanaco meat (from left to right: meatballs, smoked, and pickled in vinegar)
Aside from the tours, there are some nearby parks to explore. We walked to a meadow where both G and my foreign friend (who traveled there alone later) sat to contemplate life and found some clarity.
About a 1.6km walk from the town center
Walking back to the hotel from the meadow, we passed a square commemorating the Falklands War (known as the Malvinas War in Argentina). The loss of the 1982 war is a collective trauma in their national memory, and the sovereignty issue is a point of national consensus. If you’re interested, it’s worth reading up on this history.
The Malvinas are “personified” here as a female warrior
#💰 Summary This “Special Forces” glacier trip cost a total of approximately: ¥7000, including round-trip flights to El Calafate, accommodation, food, tours, and airport transfers.
That’s all for this one. 👋 See you in the Ushuaia and Bariloche chapters.
summary:
🔥 Key Takeaways

  1. After this trip to Argentina, I’ve realized that to truly save money, I must force myself to become a meticulous planner for future long-distance travels;

  2. Be more direct when speaking; foreigners often don’t understand subtext or “hidden meanings”;

  3. The glacier is absolutely worth seeing; the actual sight is incredibly breathtaking and worth every penny;

  4. The local roasted lamb is excellent; it’s a paradise for carnivores;

  5. When flying in South America, always leave plenty of buffer time;
title: Argentina Travelogue (III): Spending $1000  in 48 Hours: My Perito Moreno Glacier 'Special Forces' Trip

content:
🧳 Preparation The trip to Iguazu Falls was finalized two months before we even set foot in Argentina. It was the first part of our itinerary to be locked in, so we reaped the rewards of early planning—cheap flight tickets.
We bought tickets for Flybondi, only to discover afterward that “Red Flag” posts about them are all over social media. Apparently, this airline has a reputation for being quite chaotic. But what could we do? The tickets were already paid for and non-refundable, so we just kept a positive attitude and hoped for the best🤷.
As it turns out, airlines in Argentina—and South America in general—aren’t particularly reliable. This was just the beginning of our “stories” with various airlines. Over the two months following our purchase, Flybondi changed our flight time three times, eventually delaying the departure by eight hours. Fortunately, we hadn’t planned to do much on our arrival day anyway; otherwise, our short trip (one night and one full day) would have been severely compromised.
Transportation in Iguazu mostly consists of buses or driving. Our guesthouse offered a shuttle service, so we coordinated directly with their staff for pickups. If you prefer the bus, there are plenty of guides available online. I recall the round-trip price between the airport and our guesthouse for four people was 38,000 Pesos (if I remember correctly).
🗺️ Itinerary We saw some travelers online sharing that you can hire a private car to go to the Brazilian side of the falls and back on the same day without a visa. However, we also saw posts mentioning random spot checks. Since three out of the four of us hold Chinese passports and none of us had a Brazilian visa, we decided to play it safe. We chose to visit only the Argentine side of the falls and skipped the Brazilian side.
Our plan: Rest well after arriving at the guesthouse, head to the falls at 8:00 AM the next day, and catch a 3:30 PM flight back to Buenos Aires.
Tickets were purchased online via the official website, costing 35,000 Pesos per person for foreign tourists. A friend accidentally bought an extra ticket thinking identity info was required for each, but it turns out you just need the QR code. Besides standard tickets, you can also book various “tours” through travel sites depending on your needs.
Official ticketing website: https://ventaweb.apn.gob.ar/reserva/parques
🏞 Reaching the Falls The Argentine side of the falls is located within Iguazu National Park. Once inside, you can take a small train, which requires an additional fee. The train follows a schedule, stopping at a few stations along the way, with the final stop being the “Garganta del Diablo” (Devil’s Throat) station.
After getting off the train, it’s a 15-20 minute walk to reach the falls. The path crosses several rivers, and if you’re lucky, you’ll spot local wildlife, like the Coatimundi (commonly known as a Coati).
You’ll also see various butterflies, such as the “88 Butterfly.” While we were waiting to board the train to leave, one of these butterflies even landed on my bag.
Truly, the world’s widest waterfall is breathtaking. Looking down into the abyss, you feel as though you could be swallowed by the rushing torrents at any moment.
It’s worth noting: if you go to Iguazu, keep your food well-stored and do not feed the local animals. As we were leaving the falls, the Coatis seemed to have memorized the train schedule, knowing that passengers often have stray food. During the passenger changeover, they scurry onto the seats to scavenge and even get into fights with each other.
Coatis have a very keen sense of smell, which can lead to aggressive behavior (like scratching or biting tourists to grab food). While my friend and I were sitting on a wooden bench waiting for the train, a Coati walked right up to her bag and started sniffing—she did indeed have a pack of candy inside. My friend thought it was cute and didn’t move at first, but a local woman nearby quickly pulled her away, signaling us to stay clear of these animals.
🍚 About Dining I don’t remember much about what we ate in Iguazu because our stay was so brief. Due to the flight delay, we didn’t take off until night. Dinner was basically instant noodles cooked at the guesthouse. By the time we reached the airport and finally made it to Iguazu, it was nearly 2:00 AM before we got to sleep. We could hear the wind howling outside in the middle of the night and feared it would rain the next day, but it turned out to be perfectly sunny—what a relief!
Breakfast was eaten at the entrance of Iguazu National Park. There are cafes, bakeries, and snack shops near the gate. The food wasn’t great, but it was edible. There are also several souvenir shops and stalls selling local handicrafts nearby.
The only proper restaurant meal we had was after our tour. Since we didn’t do the “Great Adventure” boat ride into the falls, we just took the train to the main lookout and left, so we had plenty of time. If you have the energy, you can get off at the middle station and hike the Upper or Lower circuits.
We heard there was a great local fish restaurant nearby. Since we finished early, were starving, and knew the airport wouldn’t have many options, we paid our shuttle driver a little extra to take us to the restaurant and then to the airport. The restaurant is shown below.
We ordered a grilled fish and a risotto, among other things I’ve forgotten. Overall, it was quite good. The grilled fish featured a passion fruit sauce combined with other roasted vegetables. My only complaint was that the fish skin was a bit too charred and bitter. For four people, including the default 10% tip, it came to about 30,000+ Pesos per person.
💡 Important Notes We went in early-to-mid November. Personally, I didn’t encounter many mosquitoes, but another group of friends went the very next day and reported being swamped by them. I suspect it depends on exactly which trails you take.
If you are visiting during the local spring or summer, definitely bring mosquito repellent. The mosquitoes there are fierce. I got bitten once in Buenos Aires; I woke up in the middle of the night to one buzzing in my ear. I slapped it—leaving a massive bloodstain—and my hand swelled up for nearly a week.
Additionally, the path to the falls is an iron catwalk. Halfway through, we encountered a large foreign man who had tripped and gashed his forehead. He was sitting on the side with blood streaming down. Please be careful while walking!
Summary That’s it for Iguazu for now! See you in the next post👋.
summary:
🧳 Preparation

The trip to Iguazu Falls was finalized two months before we even set foot in Argentina. It was the first part of our itinerary to be locked in, so we reaped the rewards of early planning—cheap flight tickets.

We bought tickets for Flybondi, only to discover afterward that “Red Flag” posts about them are all over social media. Apparently, this airline has a reputation for being quite chaotic. But what could we do? The tickets were already paid for and non-refundable, so we just kept a positive attitude and hoped for the best🤷.
title: Argentina Travelogue (II): Stepping into Iguazu, Enveloped by Roaring Waters, Lush Greens, and Wild Creatures

content:
The Argentina series is expected to be quite lengthy, so I’ll be breaking it down into several posts. The journey covers the capital, Buenos Aires, Iguazu on the Brazilian border, El Calafate in Patagonia, Ushuaia—the gateway to Antarctica, and Bariloche, often called South America’s Little Switzerland. My posts will essentially cover everything from food and accommodation to transportation and general survival in Argentina.
This first installment focuses on my pre-trip preparations and my memories of the capital, Buenos Aires. I want to document as much as possible, so bear with me—it’s a long one.
Note: This article reflects my personal views and totals approximately 12,000 words.
I. Planning & Preparation I started planning the trip in October. Originally, I was dying to go to Mexico because early November coincides with the Day of the Dead. I was in Chiang Mai around that time last year and happened to watch Coco, which left me completely obsessed with experiencing the festival firsthand.
Mexico’s visa policy allows entry for those holding valid US visas, long-term Schengen visas, or Japanese visas valid for three years or more. However, I’d heard stories of people being deported even with the three-year Japanese visa (which turned out to be true—a girl I know later tried to enter Mexico from Argentina with that exact visa and was sent back). Plus, for South America, transiting through the US offers more flight options and cheaper prices. So, I tried my luck and applied for a US visa again. Well, I was rejected. Again.
It’s honestly kind of hilarious. If you’re a woman and don’t have much self-confidence, go apply for a US visa. The rejection reason is almost always the same: they don’t think you have enough “binding ties” to your home country, implying you’re likely looking for a green card marriage. When I told my friends back home, they joked that my style and vibe alone make me look like someone heading out to do exactly that—I was literally laughing through the tears. Later, chatting with V in Argentina, she mentioned how expensive New York is. I told her I had stubbornly tried for the visa again and failed, jokingly saying the US Immigration Bureau must have known I was broke and was just trying to save me money lol.
I asked V if green card marriages were really that common in the US. Her answer was a resounding yes. She told me that since many local men struggle to find partners, there’s an entire industry built around it. It’s a bit like the “Vietnamese bride” phenomenon—on a global scale, the logic is similar. Population flows with the economy. As women in developed countries become more self-aware and selective, men who still have traditional partner needs but average means look toward other countries.
Back to the visa: despite the US rejection, I had a backup. I had bought tickets for a conference where the organizers partnered with the government to provide a one-month visa waiver—similar to the waiver granted to US visa holders. This waiver let me stay for a month. Since it was too late to apply for a Mexican visa, I scrapped the Day of the Dead plans and flew straight to Argentina.
China Eastern recently launched a Shanghai-Auckland-Buenos Aires route, but the timing didn’t work for my schedule. I chose Turkish Airlines: Shanghai to Istanbul, a 17-hour layover, then Istanbul to Buenos Aires with a stop in São Paulo. Total travel time: 12 + 17 + 18 = 47 hours. In all my years of flying, this was the first time my feet actually swelled up. It was funny—on the second leg from Istanbul to BA, I tried to change shoes and wondered why they felt so tight. I realized it wasn’t the shoes; my feet had turned into little steamed buns.
I made a mistake. Before the trip, a friend warned me about poor circulation and the risk of blood clots on long flights. So, I bought compression socks for the first time. However, I think the swelling was actually caused by wearing them wrong. I bought the footless kind (ankle to thigh). I later learned that due to gravity, hydrostatic pressure is highest at the ankle. Compression socks should cover the ankle, not have the cuff digging into it. Naturally, I did the latter. Learn from my mistake: if you’re traveling long distances, don’t get fancy and end up making things worse.
The green part is my socks🤣
This was my first time flying Turkish Airlines (I took China Southern to Turkey previously). Honestly, the experience was great. The food was good, and the amenity kit included socks, slippers, a toothbrush, toothpaste, and an eye mask. Also, because my layover was so long, Turkish Airlines provides free meal vouchers every five hours for connecting passengers. You just go to the service desk and then take the voucher to the food court. It’s mostly fast food—pizza, sandwiches, burgers—but you can’t complain when it’s free.
The free airport transit meal they were offering included an extra piece of bread for me to try! 🤣
Additionally, Turkish Airlines provides 4 or 5-star hotel stays for layovers exceeding a certain duration, but our itinerary didn’t meet the rules. Hotel opportunities are fewer and come with more constraints, and you need to leave the airport and apply for a Turkish e-visa. If you’re interested or your schedule fits, you can check the Turkish Airlines official website or contact their customer service directly.
Transiting through Istanbul reminded me of my first time traveling abroad. I remember being at this airport with no internet, sleeping on the floor, and sprinting for ten minutes from one end to the other to catch a flight—it feels like a lifetime ago. Two years later, the difference is that I’m no longer afraid to travel alone, and this time I wasn’t alone anyway. Because of those past struggles, I knew the layout of the airport like a fish in water. For instance, while waiting with my friend, I mentioned how I once slept outside security. Because of my previous experience going to the wrong gate, I knew exactly which areas had lounge chairs, charging ports, and fewer people. I even walked to the old gate and described to my friend how I once navigated the entire airport to make my flight. All those memories felt so vivid yet the feeling was completely different—that’s growth.
Since we were heading to Argentina, there was some lingering anxiety—not about South American safety (opinions on that vary wildly, but friends in BA told me Palermo was fine)—but about whether my special waiver letter would actually get me through immigration.
A week before leaving, I heard that a colleague of my friend (Russian or Serbian) was flying Turkish Airlines from Belgrade to BA. During his Istanbul layover, the airline refused to let him board the second leg despite him providing proof of accommodation. The poor guy ended up “healing” his broken heart in Thailand instead. Then, two other people I know were questioned three times: at check-in, security, and the gate. The staff scrutinized their flight and hotel booking dates to verify their “intent.” Luckily, they argued their way onto the plane.
Naturally, I was terrified we’d get rejected at the gate too. But it was much smoother than expected. Leaving Shanghai was a breeze; the check-in agent showed my waiver to a Spanish-speaking colleague and cleared me immediately.
I think part of it was that my documentation was solid, and the Argentine Immigration office had conveniently posted about the special waiver on their website just days before. If the airport staff don’t know the rules, they won’t let you through, as airlines face heavy fines for transporting passengers who get deported. Still, there was one last check before boarding where I had to show proof of accommodation and a return flight. My internet was acting up, and I was so nervous that my hands were shaking as I showed the documents to an agent whose English was limited. Looking back, it was pretty dramatic for no reason.
Our flight had a stop in São Paulo. I saw people getting off and thought we had to deplane too, as is common in China. Plus, a friend had told me a horror story about Brazilian ground staff being so incompetent that he missed his connection. Wary of South American efficiency, I double-checked with a flight attendant and found out we stayed on board for the BA leg. The 90-minute wait on the tarmac was pretty miserable though—you’re just stuck there, and the smell of refueling isn’t exactly great. I’m glad my return flight takes a different route.
II. Buenos Aires: First Impressions I didn’t expect to love Buenos Aires (BA) as much as I did. The reason is simple: Jacarandas. They made me feel like this was “my” city. I landed at BA in the early hours of the 6th, so I didn’t see much besides the dark outlines of the city.
From Ezeiza Airport to the city center, it’s about a 40-minute drive. Taxis might take the “scenic” route, so I recommend Uber, DiDi, or Cabify. Uber is usually the priciest; Cabify is more localized. It’s worth comparing.
At first, looking out the car window, the road from the airport looked like the urban sprawl of Southeast Asia. But once we hit the city—specifically Palermo—it felt very European. The plane trees lining the streets and the vintage architecture actually gave me a massive flashback to Nanjing. I posted a photo on WeChat with the caption “I’m in Nanjing,” and half my friends actually thought I had already flown back to China.
*The street view is really similar to Nanjing *
I discovered the city was covered in Jacarandas one day while walking to the supermarket with my friend V. We turned a corner onto a main road, and a canopy of purple hit me. Under the sunlight, the lush green leaves paired with thick purple blossoms against the bright blue sky and pale yellow walls were breathtaking. It reminded me of the flame trees in Chiang Mai—but while those are full of “life-orange” energy, the Jacarandas of BA are all about “elegant-purple” tranquility.
love it💜
III. Living in Buenos Aires: Food, Housing, & Transport 🏠 Accommodation Palermo is the neighborhood all the locals recommend. The streets feel European—cobblestones, classic architecture, and sidewalk cafes/bars that give the area a distinct vibe. The famous writer Jorge Luis Borges grew up here. Our Airbnb was right near a restaurant mentioned in his stories; we passed it every day but somehow never managed to eat there lol.
With Argentina’s inflation, prices are skyrocketing. However, while food prices now rival or exceed those in Europe, housing is still relatively cheap—especially Airbnbs. We stayed in Palermo for about 100–200+ RMB per person per night. To put that in perspective, a decent dinner out will easily cost you 20,000+ Pesos (over 100 RMB) per person. However, the issue with Airbnbs is the soundproofing. Honestly, I think it’s a city-wide problem with the older buildings.
I started in a hostel my friend booked for an event; the room was like a courtyard setup, and I could hear every single person walking or chatting outside. One morning, I woke up to the surround-sound snoring of the guy in the next room. After my trip to Iguazu, I ditched the free housing and moved in with two other friends. I thought the hostel was just a one-off, but the Airbnb we moved to—which was literally at street level—was no better. You could hear pedestrians’ conversations through the window as if they were in the room with us.
Later, I visited V at her apartment in Palermo Hollywood (a pricier area). She complained about the same thing. I was sitting on her sofa working while she napped upstairs, and I could hear the neighbors chatting outside with crystal clarity. Sadly, I don’t speak Spanish, or I would have definitely eavesdropped.
🛍️ Shopping They say food is heaven, and I take that literally. Because BA is expensive and my Airbnb had a kitchen, I picked up my chef’s knife again. I’m not exaggerating: 70% of my luggage was food—instant noodles, rice vermicelli, sauces, and miscellaneous “useful” snacks.
My friends mocked me at first, thinking I overpacked. They were wrong. I brought a box of dried plums (hua mei), which became a crowd favorite. Then there’s my friend “Sister F,” the typical “big talker but weak stomach.” She thought bringing 20 packs of noodles was ridiculous, arguing we should be eating out. But by the end, she was the one craving noodles the most after getting tired of all the meat.
There was one hilarious night when Sister F wanted to hit the bars. We went out, had one cocktail each and some fried mushrooms. As soon as we stepped out, she turned pale and told me she couldn’t walk. I was worried she was going to keel over, but I also really needed to find a bathroom. I asked if she could hang on, but she was dizzy and nauseous. The other three of us were fine. Since she’s notoriously fragile when it comes to alcohol, I ended up piggybacking her for a stretch. The sight of it made her laugh so hard she regained enough strength to stumble home. Once back, she felt sick again until she ate one of my dried plums. Pro-tip: dried plums are a lifesaver for nausea.
Grocery shopping became my main entertainment. Supermarkets and wet markets are the best way to understand local life. I loved wandering around Coto because it’s affordable and has everything. Most supermarkets sell pre-cut meat, but at Coto, you can pick your cut and have the butcher slice and weigh it for you. I also tried Jumbo and some Chinese grocery stores, but Coto won on variety and price.
If you want cheap produce, the local fruit and veg shops are better, though quality varies. Some sell stuff that’s clearly past its prime, so you have to be picky. If you’re not a total carnivore, finding high-quality fresh veg in BA requires some research.
![](Asadao + mix salade.png) classic combination，Asadao + mix salade
Here’s the breakdown on grocery prices: Meat is relatively cheap. I got a 1kg ribeye for 16,000 Pesos (about 80 RMB). Pork bones and ribs are a steal—700g of ribs cost me about 10–15 RMB. Milk is also cheap, similar to Europe. Interestingly, the fat content is color-coded: green is lowest, then orange, then red. Wine is also incredibly cheap—sometimes the same price as water. The expensive stuff? Industrial processed goods like chips and candy. A bag of Lay’s (148g) was about 25 RMB, whereas it would be 10 RMB in China.
![](Fernet con cola.png) Specific drink，Fernet con cola
My biggest complaint? The coffee. I expected greatness from a country that neighbors major bean producers, but the coffee in BA was generally terrible. I usually drink black coffee, but I was forced to switch to milk-based drinks because the black coffee was so bad. And even the lattes were hit-or-miss—the foam was often coarse and soapy rather than silky.
Overall, except for the meat, my friends and I gave BA food a pretty low rating.
🚶 Safety & Transportation If Istanbul is the city of cats, Buenos Aires is the capital of dogs. You see professional dog walkers everywhere—sometimes one person wrangling 15 dogs at once. It’s a spectacle. But while BA loves dogs, not all owners pick up the poop. Walking the streets (especially at night) is a constant game of “dodge the landmine.”
Auntie was absolutely overjoyed!
Speaking of safety, we developed a weird theory: the more dog poop there is, the safer the neighborhood. It means people have the leisure time to walk their pets. My friends who go for morning runs in the park confirmed there’s poop everywhere, and Palermo is the same.
Common travel advice says to stay in the city center for safety, but in BA, it’s the opposite. The city center (near Plaza de Mayo) can get sketchy, especially at night. Palermo is the gold standard for safety. It’s where the middle/upper class, expats, and young professionals live. There’s a heavy police presence; seeing security guards in bulletproof vests is normal. That said, it’s not perfect. I saw a security guard chasing a thief once, and V told me about a guy who got punched and robbed in broad daylight.
For transport, we mostly used apps. Uber is okay, but Cabify and DiDi are popular too. Base fares start around 15 RMB. Because of the one-way street system and constant red lights, walking is sometimes faster than driving. I also tried moto-taxis (booked via apps) twice. They provide helmets and are faster at weaving through traffic. That said, they aren’t nearly as “wild” as the moto-taxis in Bangkok. Thailand still holds the crown for chaotic biking 😹.
Public transport involves the SUBE card. You can get one at kiosks, though they charge a premium. I just used Apple Pay for the buses in BA—super easy.
☎️ SIM Cards & Payments My phone is a Chinese model, so no eSIM for me. In BA, I found that buying a SIM card locally is about 1/3 the price of buying one on Taobao.
I bought a Claro card at a kiosk. The pricing was baffling: V bought one for 2000 Pesos. Three days later, I took a friend to a different kiosk and it was 2000. Two days after that, it was 1700. Another two days later, 1200. It was the exact same “Tourist SIM.” But hey, this is Argentina—the land of magical realism and hyperinflation.
A 15GB + 1000 mins plan for one month cost about 9600 Pesos. Total cost (including the card) was about 11600 Pesos (60 RMB).
Digital payments are advanced, though bugs are frequent. Mercado Pago is the “Alipay of Latin America.” I brought US dollars as a backup. Since April 2025, the gap between the “Blue Dollar” and the official rate has narrowed significantly, making it easier to get dollars through official channels.
You can exchange cash at Chinese supermarkets or official exchange houses. People on the street will also shout “Cambio!” at you. Be careful with street exchanges; some people report getting shortchanged. Cash is still king in many restaurants, often getting you a 10% discount. If you worry about the Peso’s devaluation, bringing USD cash is never a bad idea.
⛰️ Sightseeing & Attractions Over my stay, I hit the big ones: the Obelisco, Teatro Colón, Recoleta Cemetery, El Ateneo Grand Splendid, El Rosedal Garden, and the National Historical Museum.
The Obelisco is the icon of the city, sitting on 9 de Julio Avenue. It was built in 1936 and has become the symbol of Argentina.
It started raining when I got off the bus, around 6 p.m.
Teatro Colón is world-class. We took a guided tour (book online). It takes about 90 minutes and shows you the incredible architecture and decor before letting you sit in the auditorium.
This is a replica; apparently, due to improper transport, a finger of the statue was broken off.
A panoramic view of the entire hall; it’s said you can even go to the top floor for an experience.
Recoleta Cemetery was a highlight. I stumbled upon it while waiting for a Mate tea class. It’s a famous cemetery for the elite, including former presidents and the famous Eva Perón (Evita). Her face is on the 100 Peso note.
![]100.png) Luckily, I still had a 100 in my wallet.
这个家族墓碑门插了一朵🌹引发了我的好奇；这里葬着 Juan Manuel de Rosas——阿根廷19世纪的强权领导人；联邦派标志性颜色是深红色，红色玫瑰花就成为了对罗萨斯本人及其政治运动 (Rosismo) 的一种强烈的、非官方但普遍被理解的致敬和怀念的符号。
I stood before Evita’s family tomb and noticed blue tickets—apparently wish tickets. The blue ones are supposedly more expensive.
The contrast between the pristine, grand tombs and the crumbling, abandoned ones is striking. It makes you wonder if these famous people knew they’d be a tourist attraction one day. Now, some tombs are just bird nests, while others have trees growing out of the cracks. Nature taking its course.
This is Domingo Faustino Sarmiento, the seventh president of Argentina, whose symbol is the bald eagle.
V and I discussed what we’d want on our own tombstones. I said “Rebellious.” She laughed and said hers would be: “If you are reading this, I will hit you.” I suggested: “If you are reading this, I am watching you.” We both cracked up.
El Ateneo Grand Splendid is the famous bookstore in a converted theater. It’s pretty, but they had just repainted when I went and the smell was overwhelming, so we just snapped a few photos and left.
El Rosedal Garden was a pleasant surprise. I joined a morning run nearby (and failed miserably, walking after 1km). The park is full of locals exercising. With the Jacarandas in bloom, green grass, and geese everywhere, it was incredibly peaceful. Just watch out for the goose poop.
Around 8 a.m., many people were running and walking their dogs.
The National Historical Museum was a bit of a letdown. My friend and I realized that Argentine history basically starts in the 19th century. The indigenous history has largely been erased. It’s a nation of European immigrants. Even our Uber driver in Bariloche later lamented how short their national history feels.
I really like this kind of media color scheme that has a strong visual impact.
It seems like celebrities being made into merchandise really has a powerful promotional effect, lol.
Based on my knowledge of ACM in Singapore, this is indeed a tortoiseshell product, a women’s accessory, where large/tall is considered beautiful.
I missed out on La Boca, which is a shame. A friend of mine wandered off the tourist path in La Boca looking for a bathroom. A local stopped him and warned him to turn back because it was too dangerous. He narrowly avoided trouble.
A corner of Chinatown
Another “brave” move: don’t wear the wrong jersey. The same friend bought a River Plate jersey and wore it in the Boca neighborhood until someone warned him to take it off. Football is life-and-death here.
Despite the warnings, I met so many kind people: a lady who helped me translate my grocery list for the butcher, and an old grandmother who asked if we needed help while we were waiting for our Airbnb host. BA has a real warmth to it.
Finally, the Mate (🧉) experience. It’s the national drink. We took a class near Recoleta. We learned the ritual: the cebador (server) prepares the tea in a mate (cup) with a bombilla (metal straw) and passes it around the circle. Traditionally, everyone shares the same straw. I didn’t mind, though I was the first one to drink anyway.
The key is the yerba (tea leaves). You fill the cup 2/3 full, shake out the dust, tilt the leaves, insert the straw (never move it!), and add hot water. We tried original, berry, and chocolate-coconut flavors. The chocolate one was surprisingly addictive.
Mate tasting class location：https://maps.app.goo.gl/N8VZ37GpwCvLCszr7?g_st=it
Final Thoughts I never expected to spend so much of this year on the road. After Berlin, Argentina was the big one. During my last few days in Bariloche, I realized I’ve found what I truly want to do, and I plan to stay in China for most of next year. My friends call me a “travel natural” because I can eat and sleep anywhere, but mentally, I’m ready for a break.
This series will be long because I want to remember everything. This was Buenos Aires; next up: Iguazu, El Calafate, Ushuaia, and Bariloche. I hope these insights help you on your own journey. Thanks for reading. See you in the next one.
summary:
The Argentina series is expected to be quite lengthy, so I’ll be breaking it down into several posts. The journey covers the capital, Buenos Aires, Iguazu on the Brazilian border, El Calafate in Patagonia, Ushuaia—the gateway to Antarctica, and Bariloche, often called South America’s Little Switzerland. My posts will essentially cover everything from food and accommodation to transportation and general survival in Argentina.

This first installment focuses on my pre-trip preparations and my memories of the capital, Buenos Aires. I want to document as much as possible, so bear with me—it’s a long one.
title: Argentina Travelogue (I): Buenos Aires, South America’s Purple City

content:
Recently, a close friend’s boyfriend came to Chiang Mai. They were planning to rent a car and drive to Chiang Rai, and invited me to join them. The itinerary was extremely simple and straightforward: the White Temple, the Blue Temple, and the Black House. Once we finished visiting the three places, we’d head straight back.
My willpower, unfortunately, was weak. I let myself get dragged into the trip, abandoning the articles I was supposed to read and the work waiting for me. And just like that, I became the shining third wheel.
We set off early this morning from Chiang Mai toward Chiang Rai. Our driver was a very nice guy named Thep. He could speak Chinese, and during our conversation he revealed that he had previously worked in Suzhou and Shanghai for a period of time, where he learned the language. Besides Thai and Chinese, he studied English in university, so he speaks three languages.
Although he modestly claimed that he only knew “a little Chinese,” his pronunciation was actually one of the clearest I’ve heard since arriving in Chiang Mai. His main limitation was vocabulary, and occasionally there were small word order issues.
While driving, Thep recommended a roadside café that he said was quite good. We stopped there for about twenty minutes before continuing directly to the White Temple.
A brief summary of our itinerary:
We left Nimman Road around 8:20 in the morning and arrived at the White Temple at about 11:30 AM. We had lunch near the temple and then drove to the Blue Temple, arriving around 1:30 PM. After wandering around the Blue Temple, we headed to the Black House and arrived there around 2:40 PM. We left the Black House at about 5 PM, stopped by a roadside hot spring for half an hour, and finally returned to Nimman around 8 PM.
After visiting the White Temple, Blue Temple, and Black House—especially the Black House—I felt a strong urge to write something down. Otherwise, by tomorrow, I might forget everything.
White Temple: Life and Death in a Cultural Crucible On the way to the White Temple, while we were stopping at the roadside café, we looked up some information about it.
At first, I agreed to join my friend and her boyfriend simply because it felt like a rare opportunity. After all, I hadn’t been to Chiang Rai yet, and I was curious to see how it differed from Chiang Mai.
But once I agreed, the social media algorithms that constantly monitor us immediately started pushing related content to me. And that’s when an interesting connection appeared.
Previously, I had seen a couple of posts on Instagram from a Ukrainian guy I knew. One of them was rather eerie yet highly artistic: a close-up shot of hands reaching out from hell, along with enlarged fragments of scattered skulls and carved faces. Because of the filter he used, the scene looked somewhat sinister.
At the time, however, I didn’t realize that the footage came from the White Temple. Remembering that post made me feel both excited and slightly uneasy about visiting the place.
The clip I had seen focused on the outstretched hands of hell and scattered skeletal carvings. I could interpret it as a symbolic reflection on life and death—it made sense. But the atmosphere on site felt completely different from the impression in the video.
It didn’t feel gloomy at all.
On the contrary, the White Temple was almost blindingly bright. Its dazzling white walls and countless silver ornaments reflected the blue sky above, making the entire structure glow with a kind of overwhelming purity.
The scenes from the Ukrainian guy’s video actually came from the section of the bridge that visitors must cross before entering the main hall.
white temple
At first I assumed that the White Temple, like many temples in Chiang Mai, must have been built centuries ago. But that assumption was wrong.
While researching during the drive, I discovered that the White Temple was actually built in 1998 by Thai artist Chalermchai Kositpipat, who both designed and funded the project himself. The temple is still under construction today and is expected to be completed around 2070.
Before visiting, I had only casually seen photos of the temple’s outer areas. But once I saw it in person, I could immediately understand why people describe it as stylish.
It combines the architectural framework of traditional temples with modern elements, while blending multiple cultural and artistic influences together.
Several details particularly stood out to me:
The “hell” imagery at the entrance to the main hall, where visitors cross a bridge symbolizing the journey of rebirth from which there is no turning back.
The black and white floor tiles forming a Yin–Yang pattern, reflecting Chinese Taoist philosophy, while the decorative motifs themselves are distinctly Thai.
It incorporates the Yin and Yang elements of Taoism
The artificial rock formations reminded me of the Water Curtain Cave from the legend of the Monkey King, and some of the rocks even resembled the shape of the Chinese character for “ghost.”
In the pond near the rock formations, there were small elephant sculptures with fish tails. The fish tail remained in the water while the elephant’s body rose above the surface in a dynamic leap. I imagine this symbolizes the idea of a carp leaping over the Dragon Gate.
Strange Rock Fountain
Between the temple and the road runs a small river, where a strange statue resembling demons and mythical beasts stands. Its color scheme strongly resembles the palette often generated by modern AI art tools. Combined with the statue’s surreal shape, it almost looks like a cyberpunk version of hell.
And many more examples.
As mentioned earlier, we didn’t have enough time to explore the entire temple complex. But even from the parts we saw, it was clear that many Chinese cultural elements were present.
Only then did I realize that the designer of the White Temple is Thai-Chinese. Considering that the temple was built only a little over twenty years ago—and is still under construction today—it makes sense that it continues to absorb new influences.
The result is a strange but fascinating cultural mixture. Traditions, religions, and artistic styles quietly overlap here. There are undoubtedly many connections and artistic ideas that I still failed to notice.
Blue Temple: The Cyberpunk Blue of Alienation It is said that the designer of the Blue Temple was once a student of the White Temple’s creator. Therefore, its design philosophy also revolves around emphasizing a single dominant color.
Among the three places we visited, the Blue Temple was the one that impressed me the least in terms of shock or novelty.
Compared with the White Temple, the Blue Temple appeared less refined. The craftsmanship felt somewhat rougher, and the complex itself was smaller in scale. I suspect financial resources may have something to do with that difference.
The most striking part of the Blue Temple is the enormous statues guarding its entrances. Anyone with megalophobia might feel slightly uneasy standing before them.
![](blue _temple.png) Blue temple
Interestingly, on that day I happened to be wearing an entirely blue outfit, which matched the temple’s color palette perfectly—layers of dazzling blues everywhere.
The Blue Temple is also still under construction. When we entered, another statue was currently being built. I suspect many of these sculptures are funded by wealthy donors, because I noticed that one of them had a specific person’s name engraved on it.
Blue temple
To be honest, among the three places, the Blue Temple gave me the most superficial impression of “life and death". Its symbolism felt direct, almost unavoidable. Perhaps this is because its artistic approach emphasizes sheer scale.
The four massive statues at the entrances loudly declare the presence of gods and hell in an almost unavoidable way. Their symbolism is extremely direct.
Another reason might be that I personally still struggle to fully accept heavily “alienated” artistic forms. The deep blue color—especially on the gigantic statues—gave me a slightly demonic feeling. The overall aesthetic felt strange and otherworldly.
Yet this eerie quality made me feel that the Blue Temple carries the most cyberpunk spirit of the three places.
On one hand, the roughness I mentioned earlier can be interpreted as a kind of populist rebellion, something less polished and more defiant—similar to punk or hippie culture, which stands apart from the refined elegance of the elite.
On the other hand, the color palette feels very similar to the visual style often generated by modern AI image tools. Much like the demon statue in the river at the White Temple, its colors seem to resonate with contemporary digital aesthetics.
Black House: Green and Black — An Altar of Animal Sacrifice The Black House had the strongest impact on me out of the three places. In fact, it was the main reason I felt compelled to write this article.
When we arrived and were about to step out of the car, it suddenly began to rain.
Perhaps it was the rain that did it—but the thin misty veil it created made the already mysterious atmosphere feel even more enigmatic.
Black House Museum
Although some people group the Black House together with the White Temple and Blue Temple as “temples”, I personally prefer to simply call it the Black House.
Architecturally, it follows traditional Thai style. The site consists of a cluster of buildings: the largest is the exhibition hall, surrounded by many smaller structures of different types. All of them are painted black.
In the past, I thought red and black was the most striking color combination: passionate red and the deepest black. Blood-red eventually darkens into black—an ending of decay and death, while the process itself remains intense and vibrant.
But at the Black House, I experienced for the first time the collision between green and black.
The black wooden buildings, built in traditional Thai style, looked almost like coffins. And surrounding them was an overwhelming sea of green. Towering trees, emerald lakes, fresh grass — everywhere life was bursting with color. Yet inside the buildings, death was carefully preserved and displayed.
Throughout the Black House complex, there are many remains of animals. The most common are cow horns. I don’t know what species they originally came from, nor whether they are real or replicas, but I strongly suspect most of them are real.
They have been assembled into various forms—chairs, towering ladders, and other strange constructions.
Besides the abundance of horns, several things initially shocked me:
Dried animal skulls hanging on the walls of the exhibition hall.
Preserved heads and skins of wolves, giant crocodiles, and pythons placed on tables.
![](main house.png)
Paintings depicting animals in violent, bloodthirsty scenes.
And later I learned that the ashes of the Black House’s creator are also displayed in the exhibition hall.
![](main house2.png)
After seeing everything, I said to my friend:
This feels like an altar of animal sacrifice.
Elephants kneeling in submission, animal heads and skins displayed on human dining tables—perhaps their bodies have already been consumed, leaving only skulls and hides.
When these images are placed alongside the realities of domination, exploitation, and humanity’s relationship with nature, it becomes difficult not to reflect. The feeling grows heavy almost unconsciously.
As for the sculptures and exhibits related to fertility worship, those are actually quite common in many museums. The Black House does have some, but not an overwhelming amount, so they didn’t shock me as much.
However, they did offer something to think about.
In this museum, the symbols representing male sexuality are extremely exaggerated: one sculpture depicts a wild man with an enormous phallus, while another shows a woman lying on top of a gigantic one.
I’ve seen many exhibits related to fertility worship before, and many of them emphasize maternal power. But the creator of the Black House clearly seemed more focused on male reproductive power.
![](Fertility worship.png)
To summarize, the Black House left a profound impression on me. It made me experience the collision between green and black in a powerful and somewhat frightening way.
Yet fundamentally, it is still a museum. Its installations are deliberately curated, so the creator’s intentions and biases inevitably shape what we see. I accept that, though I don’t entirely like it.
Still, I would recommend visiting it. Especially in the rain. The quiet atmosphere and sparse crowds add an extra layer of eerie tension, making it easy to scare yourself.
There are also several stone formations where cow horns are arranged into structures resembling ladders to the sky, evoking ancient sacrificial altars.
Another interesting detail: inside the Black House there is a small station dedicated to donations for stray cats. I’ve actually noticed many similar initiatives throughout Thailand. For example, when I buy coffee at the language school, I often see information about shelters for stray cats and dogs.
summary:
Recently, a close friend’s boyfriend came to Chiang Mai. They were planning to rent a car and drive to Chiang Rai, and invited me to join them. The itinerary was extremely simple and straightforward: the White Temple, the Blue Temple, and the Black House. Once we finished visiting the three places, we’d head straight back.

My willpower, unfortunately, was weak. I let myself get dragged into the trip, abandoning the articles I was supposed to read and the work waiting for me. And just like that, I became the shining third wheel.
title: Three Colors in Chiang Rai: White, Blue, and Black

content:
Introduction My curiosity about Da Nang, Vietnam, was sparked last year when a friend visited Suzhou and raved about the city. Later, I stumbled upon posts comparing Da Nang to Chiang Mai, which piqued my interest even further. Last month in Chiang Mai, locals told me that many people were moving to Da Nang due to the lower cost of living and the “burning season” in northern Thailand. Da Nang seemed like the next ideal haven for digital nomads.
Before heading to Argentina, I chatted with my best friends about our Lunar New Year plans. Without much hesitation, I suggested Da Nang. We compared a few other options, but nothing matched Da Nang—and given their lingering concerns about last year’s incidents in Thailand, Da Nang became our final choice. And so, our journey into becoming “sitting ducks” began.
As someone who is always on the road, I had a momentary lapse in judgment—I completely forgot how overwhelming the crowds can be during the holidays and underestimated how insane the flight and hotel prices would become. I vow: never again will I travel during peak holiday seasons!
✈️ The First Trial: Flights and Accommodations I flew to Da Nang from Chiang Mai, while my friends flew from Shanghai. We all thought we were being “smart” by waiting, but I grossly underestimated the collective power of holiday travelers and overestimated my own luck. As a “wait-and-see” traveler, the holiday flight prices gave me a humbling reality check.
Regarding our Airbnb: we ran into an extremely deceptive situation. I’m detailing it here in hopes that others can stay alert. Since one of my friends insisted on a villa and we booked our flights before finalizing accommodation, our options in Da Nang during Tết were limited. A week before our trip, seeing that most decent hotels were sold out, we settled on a homestay for about 6,000 RMB for six nights.
To our surprise, just as I landed and finished my visa process, I received a message from my friends saying the homestay had major issues—too many geckos and outdated facilities. I initially dismissed it as a standard Southeast Asian living experience, thinking my friends were overreacting. But when I arrived, they told me they had already negotiated a full refund with the landlord. As a long-time Airbnb user, I was stunned: a full refund during the peak holiday season? My friends told me they had offered to pay for the first night, but the host declined and promised a full refund. As it turns out, in a foreign land, when something seems too good to be true, there’s usually a scam behind it. Always keep evidence—we were indeed being played.
Image: Son Tra Peninsula, near Linh Ung Pagoda
Image: Son Tra Peninsula, near Linh Ung Pagoda
Image: Son Tra Peninsula, Southeast Asia’s tallest white Lady Buddha statue
Let me break down the players: The real host (A) used his agent’s identity to register the Airbnb account. We were communicating with the host (A) on the platform, but he told us to add his agent (B) on WeChat, effectively moving the communication to a third-party platform. Host A never appeared, and Agent B claimed the host was away traveling with family for the New Year. Agent B only showed us the place in person, and because the host used his own photo on the profile, my friends thought Agent B was Host A. Later, dealing with the gecko issue, Agent B delegated to another guy (C), whom I met upon arrival. C had been a middleman coordinating between my friends and the host team. He told my friends that the host agreed to a full refund, and my friends even took a photo of the translated message. So when I arrived, I simply confirmed with them that the refund was guaranteed, and we rushed to book a hotel. The booking process was rushed due to lack of availability and our exhaustion from red-eye flights. Before leaving, I added C on WeChat, thinking he could help us with cheaper bookings next time.
There were several issues here:
Language barriers leading to cognitive bias No recorded proof of C’s promise of a refund Host A’s identity was hidden, and we were all out of sync—my friends thought B was the host, while I thought C was B’s agent. None of us were right. 😂 The next morning, the host informed us he wouldn’t refund us, claiming geckos were normal and that he never made such a promise—it was just a “misunderstanding.” We felt scammed. We reported it to Airbnb, but they replied that bugs and geckos are “normal” and they couldn’t do anything if the host refused.
I reached out to C. His response confirmed my suspicions: Host A was not the person in the profile photo; he was simply manipulating the account using B’s identity. C confirmed that they had promised a full refund and even suggested that A’s behavior was dishonest and not the first time he had done this. He urged me to report the host’s lack of integrity and safety issues to Airbnb.
At that time, we were in Hoi An, a famous international trading port known for its well-preserved architecture. During the New Year, the ancient town was glowing with lanterns and crowded with tourists. The Airbnb ordeal cast a shadow over my interest in Hoi An; the bustling crowd suddenly felt hollow.
The “smoking gun” proving platform fraud came when I saw the homestay re-listed by the host for the exact same dates we had already booked. The host’s greed was his undoing. We went back to Airbnb support, highlighted the fraudulent activity, and eventually the host offered a Plan B—a partial refund for four nights. We refused, escalated the issue, and finally, the next day, we received a full refund.
To be honest, I’ve used Airbnb countless times, but this was a first. Given my stereotypes and later experiences with some locals, this kind of behavior in Vietnam felt strangely “reasonable” in a warped way.
💰 The Second Trial: Being Fleeced We were prepared for shops to be closed during Tết, but the extent of the closures was absolute. Only tourist-centric spots were open, and naturally prices were inflated. A bowl of beef pho near My Khe Beach cost 95k VND, while the same dish near Cho Con market was only 30k–50k. A lemon tea at Mixue, normally 15k, was marked up to 18k—a 20% increase.
I can understand “tourist pricing” and holiday surcharges, but this was excessive. Still, compared to domestic prices in China, 18k VND is only about 5 RMB. Let’s just treat it as a “digital red envelope” for the workers who couldn’t go home. Besides, Vietnamese law mandates triple pay for public holidays, so a 20% markup is actually relatively restrained.
But compared to Chiang Mai, Da Nang loses hands down. Chiang Mai’s commercial maturity, price transparency, and service attitude are far superior. Even as a leader in Vietnam’s tourism industry, Da Nang’s tourism management often feels like short-sighted utilitarianism—a “grab the money and run” mentality that, under the extreme pressure of the New Year, devolves into blatant profiteering.
I usually avoid tourist traps, but I was traveling with two childhood friends who insisted on them. The experience was underwhelming, especially in Hoi An.
Image: Hoi An Ancient Town, boat ride and floating lanterns
Unlike Da Nang, where you can walk out of a tourist zone into a local neighborhood, Hoi An is essentially a “dead-end” scenic destination. During the holidays, supply and demand were completely out of balance. Many people working in Hoi An aren’t locals; they are fortune-seekers from other regions. They have no emotional connection to the land—Tết for them isn’t a festival, it’s an annual harvest season. As a result, price gouging and greed become amplified here.
Image: A street in Hoi An Ancient Town
A small detail: tipping in Vietnam is not mandatory. For the basket boat ride, 20k–50k VND (approx. 6–15 RMB) is considered generous. We negotiated the price down to 200k VND for four people.
Image: Basket boat entrance
The ride includes various “tipping stations”—performances like boat spinning, photo opportunities, and singing.
Image: Coconut forest
Image: Floating karaoke station
Image: Floating karaoke station - Korean songs
My friends were first-time travelers abroad. While I had researched appropriate tipping ranges with Gemini, our boatman began playing the victim—showing us the calluses on his hands and praising China in an attempt to guilt us into giving more money. My other friend’s boatman directly demanded a 100k VND tip. I was speechless.
In an immature tourism market, excessive kindness often breaks the rules, making things harder for the next traveler. It’s not that I’m unwilling to tip—but I hate this kind of moral coercion disguised as hardship. Asking for a 100k tip when the whole ride costs only 200k is simply absurd.
Image: Commercial street in Hoi An targeting Korean tourists
We intended to visit a trendy “paddy field cafe” found on social media, only to discover it was closed. Yet while taking taxis around, I glimpsed a much truer side of Hoi An: magnificent family tombs with Fujian-style aesthetics scattered throughout the vast rice fields.
To tourists, this “proximity of life and death” might feel eerie, but to locals it represents ancestral pride—something they fiercely protect, even while squeezing extra money from tourists during the holidays.
The boatmen begging for tips at every corner might be guarding such a tomb, built with a family’s lifetime of savings. This strange mixture of extreme materialism (scamming) and extreme traditionalism (tomb building) formed the most absurd yet authentic undertone of Central Vietnam.
It reminded me of the graveyards in Arashiyama, Kyoto. The root is the same: the Confucian obsession with filial piety and final resting places. But while Arashiyama’s tombs exude Zen-like serenity, Hoi An’s tombs shimmer with the anxiety of the living.
🤢 The Third Trial: Food Poisoning Luck is precious; misfortune, however, rarely travels alone. My “iron stomach” finally gave out in Da Nang.
On our last morning, after snorkeling, we headed to Cho Con market hoping for some good food. But the market was largely closed, with only a few street vendors still operating. The environment looked questionable, but we were hungry.
Image: Cho Con market, Feb 20th, 1:00 PM
The market felt a bit like Chiang Mai, only much less hygienic.
Image: Familiar power lines and market, but cleanliness left much to be desired…
We wanted one last bowl of pho. A grandmotherly vendor signaled 50k VND for a bowl, which I suspected was a tourist price. Sure enough, 300 meters away at a proper restaurant, a bowl with premium toppings cost exactly the same.
Menu translation (top to bottom): Mỳ xíu (Char siu noodles, yellow alkaline noodles); HOÀNH THÁNH KHÔ (Dry wonton); PHỞ TÁI - BÒ VIÊN (Beef pho + beef balls); HỦ TIẾU KHÔ (Dry rice noodles).
I thought it would be soup noodles, but the vendor served dry noodles instead. Later I noticed a group of Chinese tourists who were much more cautious, confirming every detail before ordering. We were simply too eager to pay—lesson learned. 🤣
The noodles were cold, which was strange. I mixed in the vegetables and lime, but the sauce was overly sweet and cloying. The fish balls were rubbery. Not great.
Image: Dry char siu noodles
Afterward, we passed a famous old-school fish cake noodle shop, Bà Lữ.
Image: Famous fish cake noodle shop Bà Lữ
It was packed with locals. We ordered two bowls of the “all-in” fish noodle soup. The shop had raised prices for the holiday, but reasonably. The auntie running the shop was lovely and even showed us how to eat it.
Image: Fish noodle shop holiday pricing
Halfway through the meal, I suddenly felt dizzy and nauseous.
Image: The “all-in” fish noodle soup
When I ate the crab paste, the nausea hit hard. This wasn’t like the fake fish balls in Chinese hotpot—this was dense, rich, and intensely seafood-heavy.
By the time we walked ten minutes toward a coffee shop, I couldn’t take it anymore. I grabbed a taxi back to the hotel and spent the rest of the day in the bathroom vomiting.
It was a miserable night. Luckily the vomiting stopped by morning, but I was still weak, sore, and fighting a cold. I couldn’t wait to get back to Chiang Mai and collapse in bed.
I never figured out exactly what caused the food poisoning—but take my advice: always carry travel medicine.
⌛️ The Fourth Trial: Endless Queues In Da Nang, queuing is a mandatory subject.
It started with immigration.
I opted for a visa on arrival. Here are some tips: Vietnam uses loose-leaf visas for Chinese passports due to territorial disputes. You can apply for a visa on arrival (VOA) through a travel agency or online to obtain an approval letter. It’s best to print it in color. You’ll also need two 2-inch photos and cash for the visa fee at the airport.
The immigration queue took an hour.
The departure queue was even worse—about two hours.
Da Nang airport is small, similar to Chiang Mai’s airport. If you don’t have checked baggage, do online check-in. I assumed there would be self-service kiosks to print boarding passes, but there weren’t. I had to queue at the counter anyway and then wait in another long security line.
I arrived at 8:10 AM and didn’t reach the gate until two hours later. 💧
🌱 Activities 1. Confucian Anxiety at Son Tra Peninsula Because I was sick, I spent half my time resting at the hotel. We visited Son Tra Peninsula and Linh Ung Pagoda. We walked two kilometers to a small temple before taking a taxi to the summit.
Image: Sea view in front of a small temple
Son Tra is beautiful. Unlike Hoi An, which is packed with foreign tourists, Son Tra felt more local.
Image: Small stall on the mountain, 35k VND for a coconut
I noticed many temples here. Although both Da Nang and Chiang Mai are filled with temples, they represent completely different branches of Buddhism.
First, there is the difference between Mahayana (East Asian) Buddhism and Theravada Buddhism. Vietnam is the only Mahayana-majority country in Southeast Asia. The small temples I saw share cultural roots with those in Fujian and Guangdong. They emphasize transcendence and clan identity. They feel inward-looking, almost like ancestral halls, which is why they are small and solemn.
Thailand, on the other hand, is a typical Theravada Buddhist country. Temples in Chiang Mai are luxuriously gilded—a reflection of the tradition of “making merit.” They function as social centers for the community, almost like a shared living room. In contrast, Da Nang’s temples feel more like studies or ancestral halls.
Finally, Chiang Mai’s secularization feels joyful—religion is integrated with daily life, consumption, and social interaction. Da Nang’s secularization feels anxious—steeped in pragmatism and a desire for protection. It feels very Confucian, very Chinese.
In short:
In Chiang Mai, temples are golden, lively community living rooms.
In Da Nang, they are quiet brick structures—the back study that shelters ancestral souls.
One blossoms into a grand bazaar; the other retreats into a heavy clan imprint.
Image: A scooter accident on the way down
2. Snorkeling Tragedy: A Dark Comedy of “Rowing the Boat” The snorkeling was disappointing. Compared to the Maldives, it was almost nonexistent.
Our paddle board flipped before we even started because my friend’s husband was too heavy and inexperienced. Two sets of snorkeling gear sank into the four-meter-deep water.
The boatman just laughed at us. 🤷
Image: View from the return boat
3. Grab Driver’s “Commission Map” One piece of advice: don’t blindly trust Grab drivers.
I used to think local drivers were the best food critics, but Da Nang completely updated my mental model.
Local restaurants and Grab drivers operate within a commission-based ecosystem. Every restaurant recommendation likely involves a cut for the driver.
When we were dropped off at the jetty for snorkeling, the driver immediately made a phone call—almost certainly confirming whether we were his “commission customers.”
Even when drivers ask you to cancel platform orders and pay privately, it’s often part of their survival strategy. It’s difficult to judge.
Image: Tamarind-flavored blue crab, very sweet. Don’t recall the price, maybe 135k/kg.
💬 Afterword When I finally lay down in Chiang Mai, I felt saved.
Da Nang is great—but perhaps not during Chinese New Year. The city feels like a teenager: full of energy, but also restless and aggressively utilitarian.
If you ask when I’ll go again?
My answer: avoid all public holidays, or bring a much stronger stomach.
On the flight from Da Nang to Bangkok, I sat next to a German mother and daughter. The daughter shared her candies with me.
Image: Candy shared by the German girl on the plane
We chatted about our travels.
When you meet people from different countries in a foreign land and realize you’ve both visited the same places, sharing stories and impressions—it’s a beautiful moment.
Feeling exhausted from illness and the constant rushing around, I had begun to feel tired of traveling.
Yet as we landed, the daughter and her mother said to me:
“Never, never stop travelling!”
summary: This post covers practical lessons in avoiding tourist traps and navigating holiday chaos in Da Nang. It’s more than just a travelogue; it’s a hard-earned survival guide for traveling in Southeast Asia during Tết.
title: Da Nang Tết Survival Guide: How I Learned the Hard Way to Avoid Becoming a 'Sitting Duck'

content:
Introduction Arithmetization refers to the process of converting computations into mathematical objects and then proving them using zero-knowledge proofs.
Plonkish Arithmetization is a unique arithmetization method specific to the Plonk proof system. Before the advent of Plonkish, the dominant circuit representation was R1CS, which was widely adopted by systems like Pinocchio, Groth16, and Bulletproofs. In 2019, the Plonk scheme introduced a seemingly retro style of circuit encoding. However, since Plonk pushed polynomial encoding to its limits, it is no longer confined to arithmetic circuits with just “addition gates” and “multiplication gates”; it can also support more flexible custom gates and lookup gates.
In this article, we’ll dive straight into the topic, first gaining an understanding of the Plonkish arithmetization scheme, exploring the components of a circuit and the underlying logic of its operations. Finally, we’ll compare the differences between Plonkish and R1CS arithmetization(as an appendix), allowing you to appreciate their distinctions and gain a deeper understanding of the significance of Plonkish encoding.
Plonkish Arithmetic Gates Let’s begin by understanding the basic structure of a circuit and examine the components of an empty circuit in a static state. Below is an example circuit:
In the image above, “Circuit 1” consists of several parts: arithmetic gates, left and right input wires, output wires, and wire.
We’ve labeled three arithmetic gates as #1, #2, and #3, where:
#1 is a multiplication gate with inputs $x_5$ (left input) and $x_6$ (right input), and the output is $out$. #2 is an addition gate with inputs $x_1$ (left input) and $x_2$ (right input), and the output is $x_5$. #3 is another multiplication gate with inputs $x_3$ (left input) and $x_4$ (right input), and the output is $x_6$. Note: Both $x_5$ and $x_6$ serve as inputs to gate #1 and outputs for gates #2 and #3, respectively.
Beyond the visible components, there are also implicit mathematical relationships that must hold for the circuit to function correctly. These are the constraint relations that the circuit must satisfy:
